nativeRange.habitat: Lake Inle, a shallow calcareous highland lake in Shan State, Myanmar; clear, hard, alkaline water over silty substrate with emergent and submerged vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved midground plants (Vallisneria, Egeria, hornwort)
open swimming lanes for shoaling behaviour
driftwood and leaf litter (though biotope is hard-water — use sparingly; may lower pH)

diet.notes: Micro-predator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, zooplankton, insect larvae, and algae. In the aquarium accepts high-quality micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, and frozen/live micro-foods (baby brine shrimp, micro worms, daphnia, cyclops). Varied diet maintains colour and condition. Mouths are very small — food size must be appropriate.

goodWith: small livebearers tolerant of hard alkaline water
peaceful small tetras in similar water parameters
dwarf shrimp (adults generally safe; small fry may be picked off)
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete for food
fish requiring soft, acidic water (incompatible water chemistry)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Sawbwa resplendens; shows adult male with red snout and white/blue body. Check FishBase image page for exact attribution.
Wikimedia Commons image showing male Sawbwa resplendens; verify license and attribution on commons page before use.

summary: A tiny, hard-water microrasbora endemic to Lake Inle, Myanmar. Males display a vivid red snout and red caudal spot; despite the common name, it is unrelated to the South American rummy-nose tetras. Critically Endangered in the wild due to habitat degradation and invasive species. Requires hard, alkaline water — atypical for small Southeast Asian cyprinids.
careNotes: Sawbwa resplendens is a monotypic species (sole member of genus Sawbwa) and the only cyprinid in the hobby that demands hard, alkaline water in the manner of Lake Tanganyika or African Rift Valley setups — a counter-intuitive requirement for a small Southeast Asian fish. Lake Inle sits at ~900 m elevation in the Shan Highlands of Myanmar; the lake's calcareous geology produces naturally hard, alkaline, clear water. Replicating these parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, gH 10–25) is essential; keeping Sawbwa resplendens in the soft, acidic water typically recommended for small rasboras leads to rapid decline. A group of at least 8 (preferably 12+) is strongly recommended to observe natural shoaling behaviour and distribute male-to-male aggression. Males in peak condition display an intense crimson-red snout and matching red caudal-peduncle spot; females and subdominant males are pale. The red snout creates superficial resemblance to the true rummy-nose tetras (Hemigrammus rhodostomus and relatives) — an example of convergent evolution, not relatedness. Regular small water changes and stable chemistry are important; the species is sensitive to poor water quality. Temperature should remain in the cooler range (20–26°C); sustained high temperatures above 27°C are stressful. The species is Critically Endangered (IUCN) due to introduction of exotic species (primarily the carp Carassius auratus and others) into Lake Inle, eutrophication from intensive agriculture on the lake, and harvesting for the aquarium trade. Wild collection pressure is a documented concern; captive-bred specimens are preferred wherever available.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; breeding is achievable in a dedicated species tank but considered moderately challenging. A pair or small group should be conditioned with live and frozen micro-foods. Spawning occurs among fine-leaved plants or Java moss; adults will consume eggs so a mesh or plant-dense spawning medium with eggs being removed promptly is advisable. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours at 24°C; fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods for the first week before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Sexing is straightforward in mature fish: males have the vivid red snout and caudal spot; females are drabber and deeper-bodied when gravid.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Sawbwa resplendens
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy, native range (Lake Inle, Myanmar), water parameters (pH 7.0–8.5, hardness 90–357 ppm / ~5–20 dH), temperature 20–26°C, adult size (~3.5 cm), diet (small invertebrates, algae), breeding notes, and conservation status (Critically Endangered). Hard alkaline water requirement confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Sawbwa resplendens
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (max 3.5 cm TL), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Inle Lake drainage, Myanmar), and diet (omnivore, small invertebrates). Monotypic genus confirmed.

additional.notes: Aquarium Co-Op trade care guide; supplemented husbandry parameters, minimum group size recommendation, tank size, and compatible tankmates for the hobby context.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Sawbwa resplendens species profile
additional.notes: Practical Fishkeeping species-of-the-week profile; confirmed hard alkaline water requirement, Lake Inle endemic status, and Critically Endangered conservation concern.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Sawbwa resplendens
additional.notes: IUCN Red List assessment. Species assessed as Critically Endangered; primary threats identified as invasive species introductions to Lake Inle, agricultural eutrophication, and collection pressure. Used for conservation status and threat narrative in careNotes.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawning achievable in species tank with fine-leaved plants or mesh spawning mop. Adults consume eggs — remove spawning medium or parents promptly. Fry are very small; infusoria required for first week. Sexing easy in mature fish by male red snout and caudal spot.
